story:
Corinne sits at her wedding reception feeling faint, the bodice of her dress biting into her ribs where the second fitting never happened and pregnancy has filled it out. She sips water in tight, careful mouthfuls. None of it matters now, she tells her mother.

“None of it matters?” Susan stares at her as if the chandelier might fall. “On your wedding day?”

Corinne tilts her head toward the dance floor. Andrew, her brand-new husband, lurches through the lights, laugh too big, glass too full. He isn’t celebrating, she thinks. He’s numbing himself because he knows he’s made a mistake. She lists her own mistakes, flat and honest: falling for him, sleeping with him, getting pregnant and then marrying for respectability—a quaint word that, in her mouth, tastes like a trap.

A sharp cramp clenches low and hard. Corinne stands, breathes, tries to loosen the dress with her fingers. “I need air,” she says, but Susan is already moving, a hand at Corinne’s elbow, eyes fixed on Andrew.

“Sit,” Susan tells her, bustling away. “I’ll get him.”

By the time Corinne lowers herself back onto the chair, Susan returns with Andrew in tow. He leans down, the heat of alcohol and cologne between them. “You sick?” he asks, but his gaze slides past her to the long-legged girl in a red dress laughing near the band.

“As if you care,” Corinne says.

“Now, now,” Susan soothes, a smile stretched thin. “The dress is too tight. That’s why she feels faint.”

Andrew grins like a boy with a rude joke. “Cramps? Should’ve worn looser underwear.”

“I’m pregnant,” Corinne says evenly. “With your child.”

He snorts, then papers it over with a shrug. “Relax. I’m kidding.”

Her fist curls before she knows it. Susan catches her wrist, gentle but firm, and Andrew, still smirking, drifts back toward the music.

Corinne folds into herself and the tears come, hot and quiet. “He doesn’t love me,” she says into her hands. “Who gets treated like dirt by her husband on her wedding day?”

Susan clings to the script she learned and taught. “He married you, didn’t he?”

Corinne lifts her head. “Because you and Dad cornered him.”

Terry arrives with the warm glow of whiskey and fatherly pride, his tie loosened, his smile ready. “What’s this?” He looks from Corinne to Susan. “What’s happened?”

“You forced him,” Corinne says. “Didn’t you? You told him to do the right thing.”

Terry’s eyes flicker. He loves the truth; he hates what it does. “I pressed him,” he admits quietly. “I told him to be a man and take responsibility. We thought time—” He glances at Susan. “We hoped time, and the baby, would bring him along. Make him into a husband. A father.”

Hearing it said aloud dissolves what’s left of the fairy tale like sugar in water. Corinne feels the cramp ease, replaced by a steadier ache. No one is coming to rescue this story. She must.

“Get the minister,” she says. “I want out.”

“Corinne,” Susan whispers, horrified.

“Terry.” Corinne looks at her father. “Please.”

Andrew is called back and there is a noisy, graceless argument—Andrew protesting, Susan shushing, Terry insisting, Corinne refusing to lower her voice—interrupted only when the master of ceremonies appears at the edge of the fray, smile fixed. “Shall we begin the speeches?”

“There will be only one,” Corinne says.

Terry searches her face. “Are you sure?”

“If I keep pretending, it’ll get worse,” she says. “I won’t.”

Terry thinks of deposits and centerpieces, of gossip, of his daughter as a little girl with skinned knees, standing up again. He takes out his phone and dials the minister. He turns from the music and speaks in a low voice, nodding. When he hangs up, he cups Corinne’s cheek and smiles sadly.

“It can’t be undone tonight,” he tells her. “There’s a process. You’ll have to separate and wait. But you can come home.”

Corinne throws her arms around him, the lapel of his jacket scratchy against her face. He smells like aftershave and relief.

Terry straightens his tie and walks to the microphone. His voice carries, calm and firm. He thanks everyone for coming. He says the evening is ending early. He asks the band to stop.

For a heartbeat there is only shocked silence. Then the room empties in a practiced hush: chairs scrape, whispers rise, the polite rustle of people deciding not to look. Most guests slip out quickly. Andrew and his family remain, bewildered and blustering, standing amid abandoned glasses and wilting flowers as the reception dissolves around them.

Corinne breathes. The noise, the dress, the lies, the forced smiles—one by one they fall away. She ends the day as bravely as she can: not with a toast, but with a choice. For the first time since morning, relief lands in her chest, clean and cool.
